speaker: interviewer
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, very good. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, [POINTING] this is a part in Berlin where I grew up. Alexanderplatz is close by here. This is the cemetery where my mother is buried. She's still there, her grave is still there. It's a very big Jewish cemetery. And this is part of the area where I grew up as a youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This [POINTING] is my father in 1916 as a German soldier. And every Jewish soldier had a tallis, a prayer shawl right in his knapsack. That's the way my mother recognized him. And in this picture here-- this is 1928-- This is my first mother, my father, and myself when I was two years old. And down here is my first day in school in 1932, in April. And every youngster who went to school at that time had a little bag here full of candies. It was a rule. And here again, this is a cut off from the picture over there. I am two years old in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, [POINTING] this Boulougne sur Mer. That was the first labor camp we were in. It was an experiment, and it never really worked. Then we traveled continuously over here, and over here, number two, is the assemblyment camp at Mechelen, or Malines, in Belgium, where all the Belgian Jews were sent to Poland from there. And thus, we travel again here, going down here. Here, number three is a crossing in 1938, after the Kristallnacht, when my father and I crossed over there, this going toward Poland. And as we go down here, this is an excerpt out of a paper, out of Primo Levi, who wrote all the books, who I've met in Auschwitz itself, who couldn't emotionally cope with the aftermath and the injustice in life and committed suicide. And then as we go over here, this is me in 1942. I was 16 years old. And down here is the famous J, or the yellow star, which Jews had to carry. And as you go over here, this is a red triangle, or a political prisoner in any camp had that kind of a coloring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Louis, where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brussels. This is out of an identification card. OK, [POINTING] this is the concentration camp, Auschwitz. It's called a registration. My friend out of Antwerps, who was in Auschwitz several times is the president of the Buchenwald Association in Europe. He brought it back for me. This is a copy. The original is there. It just states my original name, Posner, Ludwig. And then of course Israel, because every German Jew, the male was called Israel, and the women were called Sara. There was a Hitler decree again, and so on and so forth. Next to it as we go over here it's a report on a punishment. The chief SS of the labor camp in Auschwitz itself, of the labor commandos, caught me walking slow, and the commando unit chief, the kapo, had to report me. And the punishment consisted of 25 lashes and five nights in the Stehbunker. A bunker is about a meter by meter where you stand with about six other fellows all night, and then the next morning you go back to work. Not pleasant. And as we go down here, and these are the-- oh, these are the estimates of the Jewish losses in Europe of all the camps, of all the people who passed away and so on and so forth. And as we go over here, you have a color chart of all the triangles. In other words, you have a political triangle. This was S social, or anti-social. The blacks were gypsies. The pinks were homosexuals. The green triangle with a point down was a professional one, with a point up was a lifer. And then you had the Jewish triangle, the yellow star, or people who had escaped, the red with the yellow star, the red with the yellow star, and so on and so forth. Down here you have P for Pole, F for a Frenchman. And if he was-- he was re-committed in a camp-- so this is basically a chart of all the colorings, which we had to wear on our pants, as well as on our jackets. And on-- next to it were the numbers to identify you. [POINTING] These are all original photographs from Auschwitz, photographed not during the wartime. This is aftermath. This is one of my collections. And as we go over here, you can see what an inmate looked like, or what an inmate had to wear-- the cap, the suit. You had the number and the triangle. And again here, on the side, also the number and triangle. And again here, the Slavs, and so on and so forth. And as we go down here, you see excerpts out of a book, arbeit macht frei, work makes free, the entrance in Auschwitz. The SS, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. And here is an original arm band from the Waffen-SS, which eventually will probably go to the Wiesenthal Center, but it belongs to me. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a chess set I found [POINTING] under the barracks after the liberation. I brought it back and gave it to the Wiesenthal Center, as well as a [? tefillin, ?] the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You found those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found those. And I found the tallis and the prayer book. And this all is in the Wiesenthal archives. Also, this ledger up here also belongs to the Wiesenthal Center now. I also found that under the barracks. And this here-- oh, this is for my mother, I think. Yes, this is for my mother. How she was deported as the first transport out of Berlin. These three here are very important people. One of them is Bruno Baum, the famous Austrian Communist resistance leader, underground leader in Monowitz. Rudolf Vrba is a man who went to England, escaped from Auschwitz and went to England. He wrote a book which is named, in Poland there. And this is-- this is Bruno Baum, and this is Hermann Langbein. I should rephrase that. This is Bruno Baum. Hermann Langbein was a big witness at the Auschwitz trials in Frankfurt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are original pictures from the Warsaw Ghetto, [POINTING] as well as this one underneath, as well as this one over here, which I got from a German soldier when I was in Auschwitz. And I have the originals at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I asked him if he had any pictures. And he says, ja, and I says, well, give them to me. He says, nein. I said, give them to me, at which time, he gave them to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. They were one of the POW's who went through Auschwitz. And this here on the end is a medical corps with some inmates and some nurses from the Russians who liberated us. Underneath of this are the doctors who treated us. Those were all Russian medical doctors. And this gentleman is Captain Shane, who was a KGB captain who I translated many, many things for him. Matter of fact, he took me in his Jeep for some-- I went to Treblinka, to Sobibor. He wanted to know on what the people died, and I went with him. And this is a bunch of French people. They were all Communists in Auschwitz, after the liberation. The lady friend who was mine is right here. That's Louisette, who by now has passed away. She was a very dear friend of mine. OK. [POINTING] This is a map out of a book which I acquired in 1945. It's out of a French book. There are only 2,000 books alive, wherever they are. And it is a map of all the camps, women and-- and-- and men, labor camps, concentration camps, et cetera, et cetera, which comes out of this book. That's all there is to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to show us Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Auschwitz, and all the side commandos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Auschwitz III versus I and everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me have a look. OK, Auschwitz, the main camp, is here. Birkenau is here. And Buna is over here. Or Monowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many miles are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about two miles between Auschwitz and Birkenau, and we're talking about 10 miles between Monowitz, or Buna, the main camp, and Auschwitz. That's what we're talking about.
